The **2001–02 season** was the 122nd season of competitive [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") in England.

## Events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2001%E2%80%9302_in_English_football&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Events")]

### Arsenal cruise to title glory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2001%E2%80%9302_in_English_football&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Arsenal cruise to title glory")]

In what had earlier been one of the most closely fought [Premiership](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") title races for years, [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") won the championship by a seven-point margin. Their crown was won in the penultimate game of the season when they beat defending champions [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") 1–0 at [Old Trafford](/wiki/Old_Trafford "Old Trafford"). Four days earlier, they had also won the [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") with a 2–0 victory over [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") at the [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium"), [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff").

Their top scorer, [Thierry Henry](/wiki/Thierry_Henry "Thierry Henry"), was on target 24 times in the league alone.

One downside to the season was Arsenal's failure to progress to the quarter-finals of the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League").

### Sir Alex puts off his retirement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2001%E2%80%9302_in_English_football&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Sir Alex puts off his retirement")]

[Sir Alex Ferguson](/wiki/Alex_Ferguson "Alex Ferguson") announced in February that he would be postponing his retirement as Manchester United manager by at least [three more seasons](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_in_English_football "2004–05 in English football") after he signed a new contract as manager. The news came just after United completed an impressive revival in the Premiership which saw them rise from ninth to first in the space of two months.

On 8 December, United stood ninth in the Premiership – 11 points behind [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C."), who had a game in hand – and had lost five of their seven previous games in the aftermath of a 1–0 defeat at home to [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C."). Even a [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League") place appeared beyond reach. An eight-match winning run, however, saw United cruise to the top of the Premiership and they were soon challenging a three-horse race with Liverpool and Arsenal. They remained in the title race until the penultimate game of the season, when they lost 1–0 at home to Arsenal and surrendered the title crown to their opponents.

United's failure was largely put down to the inability to find a suitable replacement for [Jaap Stam](/wiki/Jaap_Stam "Jaap Stam"), the Dutch central defender who had been sold to [Lazio](/wiki/S.S._Lazio "S.S. Lazio") for £16.25 million just after the start of the season. Another disappointment was the failure of [Juan Sebastián Verón](/wiki/Juan_Sebasti%C3%A1n_Ver%C3%B3n "Juan Sebastián Verón") at £28.1 million the most expensive player ever to sign for an English club, to never live up to his hefty price tag. On a positive note, Dutch striker [Ruud van Nistelrooy](/wiki/Ruud_van_Nistelrooy "Ruud van Nistelrooy") lived up to his £19 million price tag by scoring 36 goals in all competitions during his first season at Old Trafford, breaking the club record that had been set by [Dennis Viollet](/wiki/Dennis_Viollet "Dennis Viollet") [40 seasons earlier](/wiki/1961%E2%80%9362_in_English_football "1961–62 in English football").

After the season concluded, rumours began to circulate that Ferguson was about to break the English transfer record once again and sign [Leeds United](/wiki/Leeds_United_F.C. "Leeds United F.C.") central-back [Rio Ferdinand](/wiki/Rio_Ferdinand "Rio Ferdinand") in the hope that his side would return to their winning ways in [2002–03](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_in_English_football "2002–03 in English football").

### Ipswich fall from grace

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2001%E2%80%9302_in_English_football&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ipswich fall from grace")]

A year after qualifying for the UEFA Cup and earning [George Burley](/wiki/George_Burley "George Burley") the Manager of the Year award, [Ipswich Town](/wiki/Ipswich_Town_F.C. "Ipswich Town F.C.") dropped back into [Division One](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division"). The [Suffolk](/wiki/Suffolk "Suffolk") side had looked doomed by [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") after winning just one of their first 18 Premiership games. Then came a turnaround in form which saw them win seven out of eight fixtures and climb to 12th place, suggesting that they were safe. But another decline set in, and this time Ipswich were unable to halt it. Their relegation was confirmed with a 5–0 defeat away to runners-up Liverpool on the final day of the season.

Joining Ipswich in Division One would be already doomed [Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County_F.C. "Derby County F.C.") and [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C."), both going down after six seasons in the Premiership, the last of which had seen three different men take charge of the team.

### West Brom and Birmingham back in the big time

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2001%E2%80%9302_in_English_football&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: West Brom and Birmingham back in the big time")]

[West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C.") and [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City_F.C. "Birmingham City F.C."), who had last played in the top flight in [1985–86](/wiki/1985%E2%80%9386_in_English_football "1985–86 in English football"), were finally promoted back to the top division with Albion as Division One runners-up and Birmingham as playoff winners. Albion had overtaken another local rival in [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C."), which had been out of the top flight even longer, in the race for the run for second place by winning seven of their final nine league games. Birmingham's triumph was sealed by a penalty-shoot out win over [Norwich](/wiki/Norwich_City_F.C. "Norwich City F.C.") at the Millennium Stadium. They followed champions [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C."), managed by [Kevin Keegan](/wiki/Kevin_Keegan "Kevin Keegan"), into the Premiership.

#### The Battle of Bramall Lane

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2001%E2%80%9302_in_English_football&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: The Battle of Bramall Lane")]

Main article: [Battle of Bramall Lane](/wiki/Battle_of_Bramall_Lane "Battle of Bramall Lane")

One of the seven Albion wins in their promotion run came on 16 March 2002, against [Sheffield United](/wiki/Sheffield_United_F.C. "Sheffield United F.C.") at [Bramall Lane](/wiki/Bramall_Lane "Bramall Lane"). This match would turn into one of the most ill-tempered in recent English football history, with three United players [sent off](/wiki/Penalty_card#Red_card "Penalty card"), two of them for violent conduct in the second half. The match was abandoned with Albion up 3–0 after two other United players came up injured, reducing them to six players. Ultimately, [The FA](/wiki/The_Football_Association "The Football Association") let the 3–0 scoreline stand.

### Back to back for Brighton

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2001%E2%80%9302_in_English_football&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Back to back for Brighton")]

[Brighton & Hove Albion](/wiki/Brighton_%26_Hove_Albion_F.C. "Brighton & Hove Albion F.C.") secured successive title winning promotions. This was a massive turnaround for the fortunes of the club which had between 1996 and 1998 finished second bottom of the football league and were on the verge of going out of business.

### ITV Digital crisis plunges league clubs into turmoil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2001%E2%80%9302_in_English_football&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: ITV Digital crisis plunges league clubs into turmoil")]

The collapse of debt-ridden ITV Digital in May 2002 plunged many Football League clubs into turmoil. The likes of Bradford City, Bury, Nottingham Forest, Watford, Barnsley, Lincoln City and Port Vale filed for [administration](/wiki/Administration_(British_football) "Administration (British football)"), fearful that the drastic loss of revenue would put them out of business.

Of all the troubled clubs, Bradford City's situation was the most precarious. The West Yorkshire club had debts of £36 million and had failed to meet a deadline for a takeover deal. It seemed inevitable that the Bantams, who had gone into liquidation in 1983, would endure a total collapse and lose their place in the Football League. Their only hope was for the Football League to ignore their financial plight and allow them a place in Division One for the 2002–03 season.

### Dario reaches managerial milestone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2001%E2%80%9302_in_English_football&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Dario reaches managerial milestone")]

On 20 November 2001, [Dario Gradi](/wiki/Dario_Gradi "Dario Gradi") took charge of his 1,000 competitive game in charge of [Crewe Alexandra](/wiki/Crewe_Alexandra_F.C. "Crewe Alexandra F.C.") in Division One. Gradi had arrived at Crewe in June 1983, and he reached this landmark just four months into a season which had already seen a total of more than 20 managerial changes in the Premiership, Division One, Division Two and Division Three. Crewe ended the season by being relegated to Division Two but the club's directors showed no intention of parting company with the longest-serving manager at any [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League") club.

### Wimbledon get go-ahead for Milton Keynes move

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2001%E2%80%9302_in_English_football&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Wimbledon get go-ahead for Milton Keynes move")]

Main article: [Relocation of Wimbledon F.C. to Milton Keynes](/wiki/Relocation_of_Wimbledon_F.C._to_Milton_Keynes "Relocation of Wimbledon F.C. to Milton Keynes")

Just after the end of the Division one season, a three-man panel of The FA gave permission for [Wimbledon FC](/wiki/Wimbledon_F.C. "Wimbledon F.C.") to move from their historic South London home to [Milton Keynes](/wiki/Milton_Keynes "Milton Keynes"). The move sparked outrage among Wimbledon supporters, who formed their own club – [AFC Wimbledon](/wiki/AFC_Wimbledon "AFC Wimbledon") – in response to the controversial plans of chairman [Charles Koppel](/w/index.php?title=Charles_Koppel&action=edit&redlink=1 "Charles Koppel (page does not exist)").

## League tables

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2001%E2%80%9302_in_English_football&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: League tables")]

### FA Premier League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2001%E2%80%9302_in_English_football&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: FA Premier League")]

Main article: [2001–02 FA Premier League](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_FA_Premier_League "2001–02 FA Premier League")

Arsenal won their second Premier League title in five seasons, as well as their second [double](/wiki/Double_(association_football) "Double (association football)") in the same period, after a remarkable season in which they fought with the rest of the top six for the title right up until the final stages. They scored in every Premier League game, were unbeaten away from home, and finished the league season with 13 successive wins – and also had the satisfaction of clinching the title with a win over Manchester United at [Old Trafford](/wiki/Old_Trafford "Old Trafford") in their penultimate game. Liverpool's second-place finish was their highest for 11 years, ending Manchester United's top-two run which had started after theirs had ended. United had rallied well and led the league for a while after losing six league games between mid September and early December, but dropped points in the final two months of the season left them ten points behind Arsenal at the season's end. Newcastle United finished fourth, their highest position for five years, in their third season under the management of 69-year-old [Bobby Robson](/wiki/Bobby_Robson "Bobby Robson").

Leeds United and Chelsea had to settle for UEFA Cup qualification after losing ground in the closing stages of the title race. Leeds's failure to qualify for the Champions League would trigger a financial implosion that would eventually see them relegated to the third tier and almost be expelled from the Football League within five years.

For the first time in 12 years, all three newly-promoted sides avoided relegation. Blackburn Rovers fared the best, finishing 10th and winning the League Cup, thereby qualifying for the UEFA Cup. Fulham survived relatively comfortably, though a 13th-place finish was considered underwhelming, as their being managed by the highly regarded [Jean Tigana](/wiki/Jean_Tigana "Jean Tigana") and a substantial investment in players had caused most pundits[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] to tip them to challenge for the European spots. Bolton Wanderers' strong start to the season meant that they survived in 16th place, despite their lacklustre form for much of the rest of the season.

Leicester City's final season at [Filbert Street](/wiki/Filbert_Street "Filbert Street"), their home for 111 years, ended in relegation after just five league game wins. Joining them in Division One were their local rivals Derby County, who had been promoted alongside them six years earlier. Derby had finished not far above the relegation zone in the previous two campaigns, but Leicester had been a top 10 side as recently as two years before their relegation, when they had also won their second League Cup in four seasons.

Last to go down were Ipswich Town, who had revived themselves mid-season after a disastrous start, but were unable to halt it after another slide towards the end of the campaign and any hopes of survival were ended on the final day of the season when they were crushed by Liverpool, just one year after qualifying for Europe (albeit they would return to Europe via the Fair Play Award). Sunderland, who had fallen just one place short of Europe in the previous two seasons, came just one place away from relegation.

### First Division

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2001%E2%80%9302_in_English_football&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: First Division")]

Manchester City managed to make an immediate return from the previous season's relegation, and returned to the Premier League as champions. West Bromwich Albion built on their surprise play-off appearance the previous year, achieving automatic promotion to the top-flight for the first time since 1986 at the expense of local rivals Wolves, who had looked all set for promotion just weeks earlier, but went out of the playoffs against Norwich. The play-offs were won by Birmingham City, finally earning promotion after two successive disappointments, and also returning to the top flight for the first time since 1986.

Burnley finished seventh for the second season running, only missing out on the playoffs on goal difference. Local rivals Preston finished eighth, with former Scotland manager [Craig Brown](/wiki/Craig_Brown_(footballer,_born_1940) "Craig Brown (footballer, born 1940)") now at the helm after [David Moyes](/wiki/David_Moyes "David Moyes") left for Everton.

Stockport County endured a dismal season despite the high-profile appointment of [Carlton Palmer](/wiki/Carlton_Palmer "Carlton Palmer") as manager, and fell into Division Two. Barnsley also went down, despite a play-off final appearance only two years previously. The final relegation spot was filled by Crewe, who for once were unable to pull off a survival act.

### Second Division

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2001%E2%80%9302_in_English_football&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Second Division")]

Brighton earned a second successive championship, despite the loss of manager [Micky Adams](/wiki/Micky_Adams "Micky Adams") early in the season – however, they would also have to cope with the loss of replacement manager Peter Taylor after the season ended. Reading earned the runners-up spot after four seasons in Division Two, and the play-offs were won by Stoke City who, despite this success, sacked manager [Gudjon Thordarson](/wiki/Gudjon_Thordarson "Gudjon Thordarson") immediately afterwards (leaving Reading in the odd position of being the only top six side to start the 2002–03 season with the same manager that they had at the start of the 2001–02 season).

QPR came out of administration at the end of a season where they finished a respectable eighth in their first campaign at this level since the 1960s, featuring in the race for a playoff place until the penultimate game of the season. Wigan Athletic finished a disappointing tenth after successive playoff appearances, but kept faith in manager [Paul Jewell](/wiki/Paul_Jewell "Paul Jewell") to deliver promotion in his second season at the helm.

Cambridge United were cut adrift early in the season, and never looked to have much chance of surviving. Long-time Division Two members Wrexham never recovered from a bad start, and also went down. Bury suffered a financial crisis off the pitch and the effects of this ultimately affected the team's performance and sent them down to the League's bottom tier. The final relegation spot was filled by [AFC Bournemouth](/wiki/AFC_Bournemouth "AFC Bournemouth").

### Third Division

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2001%E2%80%9302_in_English_football&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Third Division")]

Plymouth and Luton were involved in a close fight for the divisional title for the whole season; ultimately Plymouth won out, with Luton taking the runners-up spot and making an immediate return to Division Two after going down the previous season. The third automatic spot was closely contested between Mansfield and Cheltenham; Mansfield were the ultimate victors despite some poor results late in the season, but Cheltenham would win promotion anyway through the play-offs.

This was the final season where only a single team would drop out of the League into the Conference. Ultimately there proved to be little drama about who would go down, as it became obvious from the first few weeks that Halifax Town were doomed, never managing to earn enough wins to have any serious chance of survival.

## Diary of the season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2001%E2%80%9302_in_English_football&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Diary of the season")]

* 21 June 2001 – [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") sign [Norway](/wiki/Norway_national_football_team "Norway national football team") defender [John Arne Riise](/wiki/John_Arne_Riise "John Arne Riise") from [Monaco](/wiki/AS_Monaco_FC "AS Monaco FC") for £4.6 million.
* 25 June 2001 – [Newcastle United](/wiki/Newcastle_United_F.C. "Newcastle United F.C.") pay £6million to [Coventry City](/wiki/Coventry_City_F.C. "Coventry City F.C.") for winger [Craig Bellamy](/wiki/Craig_Bellamy "Craig Bellamy").
* 26 June 2001 – After a year with [Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona"), [Emmanuel Petit](/wiki/Emmanuel_Petit "Emmanuel Petit") returns to England to join [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") in a £7.5 million deal.
* 4 July 2001 – [Charlton Athletic](/wiki/Charlton_Athletic_F.C. "Charlton Athletic F.C.") pay a club record £4.75million for [Wimbledon](/wiki/Wimbledon_F.C. "Wimbledon F.C.") striker [Jason Euell](/wiki/Jason_Euell "Jason Euell").
* 9 July 2001 – Goalkeeper [Ian Walker](/wiki/Ian_Walker_(footballer) "Ian Walker (footballer)") who has spent his entire 13-year playing career at [Tottenham Hotspur](/wiki/Tottenham_Hotspur_F.C. "Tottenham Hotspur F.C."), joins [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C.") for £2.5 million.
* 11 July 2001 – [Rory Delap](/wiki/Rory_Delap "Rory Delap") becomes [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton_F.C. "Southampton F.C.")'s most expensive player in a £4 million move from [Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County_F.C. "Derby County F.C."). [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Middlesbrough_F.C. "Middlesbrough F.C.") sign [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.") defender [Gareth Southgate](/wiki/Gareth_Southgate "Gareth Southgate") for £6 million. Also on his way out of [Villa Park](/wiki/Villa_Park "Villa Park") is goalkeeper [David James](/wiki/David_James_(footballer,_born_1970) "David James (footballer, born 1970)"), in a £3.5 million move to [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C.").
* 12 July 2001 – [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") break the English transfer fee record by paying [Lazio](/wiki/S.S._Lazio "S.S. Lazio") £28.1 million for [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina_national_football_team "Argentina national football team") midfielder [Juan Sebastián Verón](/wiki/Juan_Sebasti%C3%A1n_Ver%C3%B3n "Juan Sebastián Verón").
* 17 July 2001 – [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C.") sign Canadian striker [Tomasz Radzinski](/wiki/Tomasz_Radzinski "Tomasz Radzinski") from [Anderlecht](/wiki/R.S.C._Anderlecht "R.S.C. Anderlecht") for £4.5 million.
* 20 July 2001 – [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") sell [Christian Ziege](/wiki/Christian_Ziege "Christian Ziege") to [Tottenham Hotspur](/wiki/Tottenham_Hotspur_F.C. "Tottenham Hotspur F.C.") for £4 million.
* 27 July 2001 – Manchester United sign [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_national_football_team "Northern Ireland national football team") goalkeeper [Roy Carroll](/wiki/Roy_Carroll "Roy Carroll") from [Wigan Athletic](/wiki/Wigan_Athletic_F.C. "Wigan Athletic F.C.") for £2.5 million.
* 1 August 2001 – [Fulham](/wiki/Fulham_F.C. "Fulham F.C.") sign [Dutch](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") goalkeeper [Edwin van der Sar](/wiki/Edwin_van_der_Sar "Edwin van der Sar") from [Juventus](/wiki/Juventus_FC "Juventus FC") for £5 million.
* 2 August 2001 – [Coventry City](/wiki/Coventry_City_F.C. "Coventry City F.C.") striker [John Hartson](/wiki/John_Hartson "John Hartson") joins [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_F.C. "Celtic F.C.") for £6 million.
* 5 August 2001 – [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth_F.C. "Portsmouth F.C.") goalkeeper [Aaron Flahavan](/wiki/Aaron_Flahavan "Aaron Flahavan"), 25, is killed in a car crash just outside [Bournemouth](/wiki/Bournemouth "Bournemouth").
* 8 August 2001 – Coventry City pay £5 million (a record for a Division One club) for [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C.") striker [Lee Hughes](/wiki/Lee_Hughes "Lee Hughes").
* 16 August 2001 – [Oxford United](/wiki/Oxford_United_F.C. "Oxford United F.C.") move into their new 12,500-seat [Kassam Stadium](/wiki/Kassam_Stadium "Kassam Stadium") after six years of waiting, but their arrival is soured by a 2–1 home defeat against [Rochdale](/wiki/Rochdale_A.F.C. "Rochdale A.F.C.") in [Division Three](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division "Football League Third Division").
* 17 August 2001 – Everton sell defender [Michael Ball](/wiki/Michael_Ball_(footballer) "Michael Ball (footballer)") to [Rangers F.C.](/wiki/Rangers_F.C. "Rangers F.C.") for £6.5 million.
* 18 August 2001 – The Premier League season begins with newly promoted Bolton Wanderers winning 5–0 at Leicester City.
* 19 August 2001 –
  + Former [Coventry City](/wiki/Coventry_City_F.C. "Coventry City F.C."), [Luton Town](/wiki/Luton_Town_F.C. "Luton Town F.C."), [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C."), [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C."), [Blackpool](/wiki/Blackpool_F.C. "Blackpool F.C.") and [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C.") goalkeeper [Les Sealey](/wiki/Les_Sealey "Les Sealey") dies of a heart attack at the age of 43.
  + Defending league champions Manchester United are host to Fulham in their first top flight game since 1968. United win 3–2, with new signing [Ruud van Nistelrooy](/wiki/Ruud_van_Nistelrooy "Ruud van Nistelrooy") scoring twice for United while [Louis Saha](/wiki/Louis_Saha "Louis Saha") scores both of Fulham's consolation goals.
* 25 August 2001 – [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton_F.C. "Southampton F.C.") lose 2–0 to [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") in their first competitive game at the new 32,689-seat [St Mary's Stadium](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_Stadium "St Mary's Stadium"). Bolton completed a three-match winning start to the season by beating Liverpool 2–1 at the [Reebok Stadium](/wiki/Reebok_Stadium "Reebok Stadium").
* 27 August 2001 – [Jaap Stam](/wiki/Jaap_Stam "Jaap Stam"), who recently revealed in his autobiography that [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") approached him without his club's permission when signing him three years ago, joins [Lazio](/wiki/S.S._Lazio "S.S. Lazio") for £16.25 million.
* 31 August 2001 – The first month of the league season ends with newly promoted Bolton Wanderers, tipped by many to go straight back down this season, as Premier League leaders with three straight wins including a 5–0 away win over bottom placed Leicester City. Everton, Leeds United, Arsenal and Manchester United complete the top five, while Leicester City are joined in the bottom three by Middlesbrough and Southampton. [[1]](http://www.manchesterunited-mad.co.uk/footydb/loadgen.asp?Day=26&Month=Aug&ssnno=131&teamno=356) In the race to reach the Premier League, [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C."), [Bradford City](/wiki/Bradford_City_A.F.C. "Bradford City A.F.C."), [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City_F.C. "Birmingham City F.C.") and [Norwich City](/wiki/Norwich_City_F.C. "Norwich City F.C.") are level on points at the top of Division One, with [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C.") a point behind them in sixth place.[[3]](#cite_note-6)
* 8 September 2001 – Liverpool's slow start to the season continues as Aston Villa beat them 3–1 at [Anfield](/wiki/Anfield "Anfield") in the Premier League.
* 12 September 2001 – All [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") fixtures are cancelled following yesterday's [terrorist attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks") in the United States of America.
* 13 September 2001 – [Cardiff City](/wiki/Cardiff_City_F.C. "Cardiff City F.C.") pay £1.7 million for [Stoke City](/wiki/Stoke_City_F.C. "Stoke City F.C.") striker [Peter Thorne](/wiki/Peter_Thorne_(English_footballer) "Peter Thorne (English footballer)") in one of Division Two's biggest transfer fees yet.
* 21 September 2001 – Tottenham Hotspur pay Southampton £8.1 million for defender [Dean Richards](/wiki/Dean_Richards_(footballer) "Dean Richards (footballer)").
* 27 September 2001 – [Michael Owen](/wiki/Michael_Owen "Michael Owen") signs a new four-year contract with [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C."), with a £70,000 weekly wage making him the most highly paid player in the club's history.[[4]](#cite_note-7)
* 29 September 2001 – [Tottenham Hotspur](/wiki/Tottenham_Hotspur_F.C. "Tottenham Hotspur F.C.") surrender a 3–0 home lead over [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") in the FA Premier League and end up losing the game 5–3.
* 30 September 2001 – [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C.") sack manager [Peter Taylor](/wiki/Peter_Taylor_(footballer,_born_1953) "Peter Taylor (footballer, born 1953)") after 15 months in charge. His team have finished September in bottom place (they were top of the league this time last year before a late season slump dragged them into the bottom half of the table), in the Premier League relegation zone along with Derby County and West Ham United. At the top end of the table, Arsenal lead over Manchester United on goal difference, while Leeds United, Bolton Wanderers and Sunderland complete the top five. [[2]](http://www.manchesterunited-mad.co.uk/footydb/loadgen.asp?Day=29&Month=Sep&ssnno=131&teamno=356) Wolverhampton Wanderers lead Division One, with Burnley in second place. [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C."), Norwich City, [Crystal Palace](/wiki/Crystal_Palace_F.C. "Crystal Palace F.C.") and Manchester City complete the top six. [[3]](http://www.manchestercity-mad.co.uk/footydb/loadgen.asp?Day=29&Month=Sep&ssnno=131&teamno=355)
* 6 October 2001 – A late equalizer by [David Beckham](/wiki/David_Beckham "David Beckham") against [Greece](/wiki/Greece_national_football_team "Greece national football team") at [Old Trafford](/wiki/Old_Trafford "Old Trafford") gains England automatic qualification for next summer's [World Cup](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup"). However, the game is marred by a series of [hooliganism](/wiki/Football_hooliganism "Football hooliganism") incidents in [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester") on the day among fans of Manchester United, Manchester City and Stoke City.[[5]](#cite_note-8)
* 7 October 2001 – [Jim Smith](/wiki/Jim_Smith_(footballer,_born_1940) "Jim Smith (footballer, born 1940)") resigns after six years as manager of [Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County_F.C. "Derby County F.C.") and is replaced by his assistant [Colin Todd](/wiki/Colin_Todd "Colin Todd").
* 9 October 2001 – [Grimsby Town](/wiki/Grimsby_Town "Grimsby Town") knock holders Liverpool out of the Worthington Cup 2–1 at the third round stage. This was the first ever victory at [Anfield](/wiki/Anfield "Anfield") for the Mariners. The winning goal was scored in the last minute of extra time, a stunning long-range strike by Liverpudlian [Phil Jevons](/wiki/Phil_Jevons "Phil Jevons").
* 10 October 2001 – [Dave Bassett](/wiki/Dave_Bassett "Dave Bassett") is named as [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C.")'s new manager, with [Brighton & Hove Albion](/wiki/Brighton_%26_Hove_Albion_F.C. "Brighton & Hove Albion F.C.") manager [Micky Adams](/wiki/Micky_Adams "Micky Adams") being appointed as his assistant.
* 13 October 2001 –
  + – Liverpool boss [Gérard Houllier](/wiki/G%C3%A9rard_Houllier "Gérard Houllier") is taken to hospital after complaining about chest pains, during the home draw against Leeds United. He undergoes emergency heart surgery and leaves [Phil Thompson](/wiki/Phil_Thompson "Phil Thompson") in sole control at Anfield, until April.
    - – Blackburn Rovers match their Premier League record win by demolishing West Ham United 7–1 at [Ewood Park](/wiki/Ewood_Park "Ewood Park").
* 17 October 2001 – Just over two weeks after being sacked by Leicester City, [Peter Taylor](/wiki/Peter_Taylor_(footballer,_born_1953) "Peter Taylor (footballer, born 1953)") returns to management with [Brighton & Hove Albion](/wiki/Brighton_%26_Hove_Albion_F.C. "Brighton & Hove Albion F.C.").
* 18 October 2001 – Leeds United sign midfielder [Seth Johnson](/wiki/Seth_Johnson_(footballer) "Seth Johnson (footballer)") from Derby County for £7 million.
* 20 October 2001 – Liverpool boost their hopes of a first top division title since 1990 by beating Leicester City 4–1 at [Filbert Street](/wiki/Filbert_Street "Filbert Street"), with [Robbie Fowler](/wiki/Robbie_Fowler "Robbie Fowler") scoring a hat-trick.
* 22 October 2001 – [Bertie Mee](/wiki/Bertie_Mee "Bertie Mee"), the manager of [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.")'s 1971 double-winning team, dies aged 82.
* 27 October 2001 – Aston Villa go top of the Premier League with a 3–2 home win over Bolton Wanderers.[[6]](#cite_note-9)
* 31 October 2001 – October finishes with Aston Villa top of the Premier League for the first time in nearly three years. The top five is completed by Leeds United, Arsenal, Liverpool and Manchester United. Leicester City, still with only one win this season, remain bottom of the Premier League with Derby County and Southampton also in the relegation zone. [[4]](http://www.manchesterunited-mad.co.uk/footydb/loadgen.asp?Day=27&Month=Oct&ssnno=131&teamno=356) Wolverhampton Wanderers remain top of Division One, with second place now occupied by a [Coventry City](/wiki/Coventry_City_F.C. "Coventry City F.C.") side who are aiming for an immediate return to the Premier League under new player-manager [Roland Nilsson](/wiki/Roland_Nilsson "Roland Nilsson"). The playoff zone is occupied by Crystal Palace, Norwich City, Manchester City and [Burnley](/wiki/Burnley_F.C. "Burnley F.C."). [[5]](http://www.manchestercity-mad.co.uk/footydb/loadgen.asp?Day=31&Month=Oct&ssnno=131&teamno=355)
* 4 November 2001 – Arsenal suffer a shock 4–2 league home defeat to Charlton Athletic.
* 19 November 2001 – Out of favour striker [Paul Kitson](/wiki/Paul_Kitson "Paul Kitson") scores a hat-trick for West Ham United in a 4–4 deaw at Charlton Athletic in the Premier League.
* 23 November 2001 – [Ipswich Town](/wiki/Ipswich_Town_F.C. "Ipswich Town F.C."), bottom of the Premier League after finishing fifth last season, pay Blackburn Rovers £3 million for striker [Marcus Bent](/wiki/Marcus_Bent "Marcus Bent").
* 25 November 2001 – Manchester United's dismal run of form continues with a 3–1 defeat at Arsenal.
* 29 November 2001 – [Robbie Fowler](/wiki/Robbie_Fowler "Robbie Fowler") ends a decade at Liverpool in an £11 million move to Leeds United.
* 30 November 2001 – Liverpool are the month-end Premier League leaders, with Leeds United, Arsenal, Newcastle United and Aston Villa completing the top five. Manchester United, however, occupy a disappointing sixth place – the result of a series of dismal performances in recent weeks. Ipswich Town, who finished fifth in the league last season, are now bottom of the table with just one win so far, while Leicester City and Derby County remain in the drop zone – but only level on goal difference with Southampton. [[6]](http://www.manchesterunited-mad.co.uk/footydb/loadgen.asp?Day=25&Month=Nov&ssnno=131&teamno=356) Burnley are the new leaders of Division One, having leapfrogged Wolverhampton Wanderers into second place. Norwich City, Preston North End, West Bromwich Albion and Crystal Palace complete the top six. [[7]](http://www.manchestercity-mad.co.uk/footydb/loadgen.asp?Day=24&Month=Nov&ssnno=131&teamno=355)
* 1 December 2001 – [Sir Alex Ferguson](/wiki/Alex_Ferguson "Alex Ferguson") writes off Manchester United's chances of a unique fourth successive league title triumph after the latest of several defeats sees them lose 3–0 at home to Chelsea.
* 5 December 2001 – Southampton buy striker [Brett Ormerod](/wiki/Brett_Ormerod "Brett Ormerod") from [Blackpool](/wiki/Blackpool_F.C. "Blackpool F.C.") for £1.75 million.
* 8 December 2001 – [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") lose 1–0 at home to [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C."), completing a dismal run of six defeats from seven Premiership fixtures, leaving them ninth in the league – 11 points behind leaders Liverpool, who have a game in hand.
* 12 December 2001 – After weeks of predominantly dismal results, Manchester United get back to their winning ways with a comprehensive 5–0 win over struggling Derby County.
* 13 December 2001 – Division One promotion chasers [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C.") sign [Rangers](/wiki/Rangers_F.C. "Rangers F.C.") striker [Kenny Miller](/wiki/Kenny_Miller "Kenny Miller") for £3 million after a successful loan spell.
* 22 December 2001 – Ipswich Town record only their second league win over the season by beating Tottenham 2–1 at [White Hart Lane](/wiki/White_Hart_Lane "White Hart Lane"). Manchester United's revival continues as they beat Southampton 6–1 at Old Trafford.
* 23 December 2001 – Liverpool sign [Czech](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic") striker [Milan Baroš](/wiki/Milan_Baro%C5%A1 "Milan Baroš") from [Baník Ostrava](/wiki/FC_Ban%C3%ADk_Ostrava "FC Baník Ostrava") for £3.5 million.
* 26 December 2001 – The highlight of [Boxing Day](/wiki/Boxing_Day "Boxing Day") sees Manchester United extend their revival and winning run to four games with a 2–0 win at Everton.
* 29 December 2001 – [Andy Cole](/wiki/Andy_Cole "Andy Cole") signs for Blackburn Rovers in an £8million deal after seven years at Manchester United.
* 30 December 2001 – Manchester United make it five consecutive league game wins as they beat Fulham 3–2 at [Craven Cottage](/wiki/Craven_Cottage "Craven Cottage"), with [Ryan Giggs](/wiki/Ryan_Giggs "Ryan Giggs") scoring twice.
* 31 December 2001 – 2001 draws to a close with Arsenal leading the Premier League on goal difference ahead of Newcastle United, with third placed Leeds United just a point off the top, while fourth placed Liverpool are two points off the top with a game in hand. A resurgent Manchester United are now fifth and just three points off the top, three weeks after being ninth in the league and 11 points off the top. [[8]](http://www.manchesterunited-mad.co.uk/footydb/loadgen.asp?Day=30&Month=Dec&ssnno=131&teamno=356) Burnley continue to lead the way in Division One, a point ahead of their nearest rivals Manchester City and Wolverhampton Wanderers. Norwich City, [Millwall](/wiki/Millwall_F.C. "Millwall F.C.") and Birmingham City complete the top six. [[9]](http://www.manchestercity-mad.co.uk/footydb/loadgen.asp?Day=29&Month=Dec&ssnno=131&teamno=355)
* 1 January – [Paul Scholes](/wiki/Paul_Scholes "Paul Scholes") scores twice as a 3–1 win over Newcastle United extend Manchester United's winning run to six games.
* 4 January/5 January 2002 – The third round of the FA Cup produces some memorable results. Derby County lose 3–1 at home to Division Three strugglers Bristol Rovers, which is yet more trauma for a club who are deep in the Premier League relegation mire. Leeds United, chasing the Premier League title, are knocked out of the FA Cup by Division Two side Cardiff City. Manchester United, meanwhile, beat Aston Villa 3–2 after being 2–0 down with 15 minutes remaining.
* 13 January 2002 – A 3–1 win at Southampton takes Manchester United top of the Premier League on goal difference after seven successive wins lifted them from ninth place.[[7]](#cite_note-10)
* 14 January 2002 – [Colin Todd](/wiki/Colin_Todd "Colin Todd") is sacked after three months as Derby County manager.
* 19 January 2002 – Manchester United extend their winning run to eight league games with a 2–1 home win over Blackburn.
* 22 January 2002 – Manchester United boost their attack with a £7.5 million move for [Uruguayan](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay") striker [Diego Forlán](/wiki/Diego_Forl%C3%A1n "Diego Forlán") from [Independiente](/wiki/Club_Atl%C3%A9tico_Independiente "Club Atlético Independiente") of [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina").
* 24 January 2002 – [John Gregory](/wiki/John_Gregory_(footballer) "John Gregory (footballer)") resigns after four years as manager of [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.").
* 30 January 2002 – [John Gregory](/wiki/John_Gregory_(footballer) "John Gregory (footballer)") returns to management six days after leaving [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.") when he is named as the new manager of [Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County_F.C. "Derby County F.C.").
* 31 January 2002 – January ends with top place in the Premier League going to Manchester United, who were ninth in the table less than two months ago. In second place are Arsenal, who are four points behind with two games in hand. Bracketed together, one point behind Arsenal, are Liverpool and Newcastle United. Leeds United complete the top five. A terrible month for Leicester City has left them bottom of the table and eight points adrift of safety, with Derby County just one places and two points above them. Ipswich Town remain in the drop zone despite a recent run of good results, while a mere three points separate the next seven clubs – Bolton Wanderers, Blackburn Rovers, Southampton, Middlesbrough, Everton, West Ham United and Sunderland. [[10]](http://www.manchesterunited-mad.co.uk/footydb/loadgen.asp?Day=29&Month=Jan&ssnno=131&teamno=356) The race to get into the Premier League is now led by Manchester City, who are six points ahead of their nearest rivals Wolverhampton Wanderers at the top of Division One. West Bromwich Albion, Millwall, Burnley and Crystal Palace complete the top six. [[11]](http://www.manchestercity-mad.co.uk/footydb/loadgen.asp?Day=30&Month=Jan&ssnno=131&teamno=355)
* 2 February 2002 – Manchester United's 4–1 home win over Sunderland extends their winning run in the league to nine games.
* 4 February 2002 – Newcastle United pay [Nottingham Forest](/wiki/Nottingham_Forest_F.C. "Nottingham Forest F.C.") £5 million for winger [Jermaine Jenas](/wiki/Jermaine_Jenas "Jermaine Jenas").
* 5 February 2002 – Division One leaders [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C.") pay a club record £5 million for [Preston North End](/wiki/Preston_North_End_F.C. "Preston North End F.C.") striker [Jon Macken](/wiki/Jon_Macken "Jon Macken").
* 24 February 2002 – [Blackburn Rovers](/wiki/Blackburn_Rovers_F.C. "Blackburn Rovers F.C.") beat [Tottenham Hotspur](/wiki/Tottenham_Hotspur_F.C. "Tottenham Hotspur F.C.") 2–1 at the [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium") to win the [League Cup](/wiki/EFL_Cup "EFL Cup") for the first time in their history.
* 28 February 2002 – Sir Alex Ferguson changes his mind about retiring as Manchester United manager at the end of the season and signs a new contract which will keep him at the club for at least another three seasons. His team are still top of the Premier League, followed by Arsenal, Liverpool, Newcastle United and Chelsea. Leicester City are sinking deeper into relegation trouble at the other end of the table, while an upturn in fortunes for Derby County is suggesting that John Gregory is their saviour. Blackburn Rovers, meanwhile, have little time to saviour their recent League Cup glory; they are still in the relegation zone. [[12]](http://www.manchesterunited-mad.co.uk/footydb/loadgen.asp?Day=23&Month=Feb&ssnno=131&teamno=356) The race to get into the Premier League is similarly tense, with Wolverhampton Wanderers and Manchester City leading the way but facing strong pressure from an improving West Bromwich Albion side. The top six is completed by Millwall, Burnley and Coventry City, while Birmingham City are moving closer to the playoff zone after a slow start to the season. [[13]](http://www.manchestercity-mad.co.uk/footydb/loadgen.asp?Day=27&Month=Feb&ssnno=131&teamno=355)
* 3 March 2002 – Manchester United are held to a surprise 2–2 draw at Derby County, with [Malcolm Christie](/wiki/Malcolm_Christie "Malcolm Christie"), a self-confessed United supporter, scoring both of Derby's goals.
* 13 March 2002 – [Walter Smith](/wiki/Walter_Smith "Walter Smith") is sacked by [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C.") after nearly four years as manager; they are reportedly interested in recruiting [David Moyes](/wiki/David_Moyes "David Moyes") from Preston North End as his successor.
* 15 March 2002 – David Moyes is appointed manager of Everton. [[14]](http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/football/teams/e/everton/1872234.stm)
* 16 March 2002 – Everton beat Fulham 2–1 at [Goodison Park](/wiki/Goodison_Park "Goodison Park") in their first match under the management of David Moyes. [[15]](http://www.evertonfc.com/history/winning-start-for-moyes.html) Liverpool and Manchester United both win their games today to keep the title challenge fierce. In Division One, meanwhile, Stockport suffered the earliest post-war relegation by any other team after they lost 3-1 away to Wimbledon.
* 17 March 2002 – Arsenal continue to keep up the heat in the title challenge with a 2–1 win at Aston Villa.
* 20 March 2002 – [Gérard Houllier](/wiki/G%C3%A9rard_Houllier "Gérard Houllier") returns to Liverpool after a six-month spell out. He watches his side defeat Roma in the [Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League").
* 23 March 2002 – Manchester United's title hopes are dented by a 1–0 home defeat by Middlesbrough.
* 24 March 2002 – Liverpool boost their title hopes when a last gasp goal by [Vladimír Šmicer](/wiki/Vladim%C3%ADr_%C5%A0micer "Vladimír Šmicer") gives them a 1–0 home win over Chelsea.
* 27 March 2002 – [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.") sign 6' 7" striker [Peter Crouch](/wiki/Peter_Crouch "Peter Crouch") from [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth_F.C. "Portsmouth F.C.") for £5million.
* 30 March 2002 – On the day of [Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_The_Queen_Mother "Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother")'s death, Manchester United remain firmly in the hunt for the Premier League title with a thrilling 4–3 win over Leeds United at [Elland Road](/wiki/Elland_Road "Elland Road"). However, Arsenal and Liverpool both win their games, keeping the title challenge heated.
* 31 March 2002 – The Premier League title race remains in full swing as March draws to a close. Liverpool are now top, Manchester United are second, and Arsenal are third, though Arsenal are two points off the top with two games in hand. The threat from the other top six clubs – Chelsea, Leeds United and Newcastle United – is now virtually non existent. Meanwhile, Leicester City's relegation is looking almost certain, while they and Derby County have been joined in the relegation zone by an Ipswich Town side whose resurgence in late winter appeared to have saved their Premier League status. [[16]](http://www.manchesterunited-mad.co.uk/footydb/loadgen.asp?Day=30&Month=Mar&ssnno=131&teamno=356) Manchester City lead the way in Division One, with Wolverhampton Wanderers still second but just three points ahead of nearest rivals West Bromwich Albion. Just three points separate the next six teams – Burnley, Millwall, Birmingham City, Coventry City, Preston North End and Norwich City. [[17]](http://www.manchestercity-mad.co.uk/footydb/loadgen.asp?Day=30&Month=Mar&ssnno=131&teamno=355)
* 1 April 2002 – Arsenal go top of the Premier League with a 1–0 win at Charlton.
* 4 April 2002 – [Dave Bassett](/wiki/Dave_Bassett "Dave Bassett") becomes director of football at [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C.") and he hands managerial duties over to his assistant [Micky Adams](/wiki/Micky_Adams "Micky Adams").
* 5 April 2002 - Manchester City returned to Premiership after only a season out after [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C.") suffered a 1–0 away defeat at Millwall.
* 6 April 2002 – [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C.")'s relegation is confirmed as they lost 1–0 at home to [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C."), whose crucial win keeps their title challenge alive. Arsenal remain in the lead with a 2–1 home win over Tottenham. A relegation crunch game at the Reebok Stadium sees Bolton beat Ipswich 4–1, with [Fredi Bobic](/wiki/Fredi_Bobic "Fredi Bobic") scoring a hat-trick. [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C.") secured the Division One title after thrashing Barnsley 5–1 at Maine Road.
* 21 April 2002 – [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C.") clinch promotion to the FA Premier League with a 2–0 win over [Crystal Palace](/wiki/Crystal_Palace_F.C. "Crystal Palace F.C.") which ends their 16-year exile from the top flight.
* 30 April 2002 – April draws to a close with Arsenal top of the Premier League with Manchester United and Liverpool still in contention, while Leicester City and Derby County have both been relegated. Ipswich Town occupy the last relegation place but will need to win their final game of the season and hope that Sunderland lose theirs if Premier League football is to be played at [Portman Road](/wiki/Portman_Road "Portman Road") and not the [Stadium of Light](/wiki/Stadium_of_Light "Stadium of Light") next season. [[18]](http://www.manchesterunited-mad.co.uk/footydb/loadgen.asp?Day=27&Month=Apr&ssnno=131&teamno=356)
* 2 May 2002 – 45 police officers are injured when [Millwall](/wiki/Millwall_F.C. "Millwall F.C.") fans riot at the end of the Division One playoff fixture against [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City_F.C. "Birmingham City F.C."). [[19]](http://www.bernardomahoney.com/forthcb/ootdie/articles/pibmm.shtml)
* 4 May 2002 – [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") clinch the eighth FA Cup victory of their history with a 2–0 win over [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") at the [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium").
* 8 May 2002 – [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") beat defending champions Manchester United 1–0 at [Old Trafford](/wiki/Old_Trafford "Old Trafford") to complete their second double in five seasons.
* 10 May 2002 – [Marlon King](/wiki/Marlon_King "Marlon King"), 22-year-old [Gillingham](/wiki/Gillingham_F.C. "Gillingham F.C.") striker, is jailed for 18 months for driving a stolen car.[[8]](#cite_note-11)
* 12 May 2002 – On the final day of the Premier League season, Liverpool beat Ipswich Town 5–0 to confirm second place (their first top two finish since 1991). That heavy defeat meant that the Tractor Boys’ were relegated and it also resulted in Manchester United being forced to settle for third place – leaving them outside the top two for the first time since 1991 and they also fail to finish winners or runners-up of a major competition for the first time since 1989. Sunderland confirm their survival with a 1–1 home draw against already relegated Derby County.[[9]](#cite_note-12) Bottom club Leicester City finish the season with only their fifth league win, beating Tottenham Hotspur 2–1 in their final game at [Filbert Street](/wiki/Filbert_Street "Filbert Street"), their home for 111 years, before relocation to the [Walkers Stadium](/wiki/Walkers_Stadium "Walkers Stadium").[[10]](#cite_note-13)
* 19 May 2002 – [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City_F.C. "Birmingham City F.C.") win promotion to the Premier League on penalties in the Division One playoff final, while fellow promoted side Manchester City sign [Sylvain Distin](/wiki/Sylvain_Distin "Sylvain Distin") from [Paris Saint-Germain](/wiki/Paris_Saint-Germain_F.C. "Paris Saint-Germain F.C.") for £4 million.
* 21 May 2002 – The Premier League and [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League") officials announce their opposition to [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA")'s plans to operate a [transfer window](/wiki/Transfer_window "Transfer window") system which could see mid-season transfers only permitted to take place during one month of the season. [[20]](https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/football/news-and-comment/premier-opposition-to-transfer-windows-651938.html)[*[dead link](/wiki/Wikipedia:Link_rot "Wikipedia:Link rot")*] A record eight black players are capped by England in the World Cup warm-up match in South Korea which ends in a 1–1 draw.[[21]](http://www.englandfootballonline.com/TeamBlack/PlyrsBlckMtchUsage/PlysBlckUsed.html)
* 30 May 2002 – Leicester City sell [Robbie Savage](/wiki/Robbie_Savage "Robbie Savage") to [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City_F.C. "Birmingham City F.C.") for £1.25 million.
* 2 June 2002 – England open their World Cup campaign with a 1–1 draw against [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_men%27s_national_football_team "Sweden men's national football team") in Group F.
* 7 June 2002 – A [David Beckham](/wiki/David_Beckham "David Beckham") penalty gives England a 1–0 win over [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina_national_football_team "Argentina national football team") in their second World Cup fixture. Manchester City prepare for their Premier League comeback by paying a club record £13 million for Paris Saint-Germain striker [Nicolas Anelka](/wiki/Nicolas_Anelka "Nicolas Anelka"), who spent most of last season on loan at Liverpool.
* 12 June 2002 – England secure qualification for the last 16 of the World Cup with a 0–0 draw against [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria_national_football_team "Nigeria national football team").
* 15 June 2002 – England defeat [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark_national_football_team "Denmark national football team") 3–0 in the World Cup second round.
* 21 June 2002 – Despite taking a 23rd-minute lead thanks to [Michael Owen](/wiki/Michael_Owen "Michael Owen"), England's World Cup campaign ends in the quarter-finals with a 2–1 defeat to [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil_national_football_team "Brazil national football team").
* 22 June 2002 – Newcastle United pay £8.5 million for 19-year-old [Portuguese](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal") midfielder [Hugo Viana](/wiki/Hugo_Viana "Hugo Viana") from [Sporting CP](/wiki/Sporting_CP "Sporting CP").
* 25 June 2002 – Liverpool sign [El Hadji Diouf](/wiki/El_Hadji_Diouf "El Hadji Diouf") from [Lens](/wiki/RC_Lens "RC Lens") for £10 million.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2001%E2%80%9302_in_English_football&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Retirements")]

| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion**. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=2001%E2%80%9302_in_English_football&action=edit&section=). *(December 2009)* |

9 November 2001: [Tony Ford](/wiki/Tony_Ford_(footballer,_born_1959) "Tony Ford (footballer, born 1959)"), 42-year-old midfielder with one of the highest appearance records of any postwar English footballer, retires from playing after leaving [Rochdale](/wiki/Rochdale_F.C. "Rochdale F.C.") and following their manager [Steve Parkin](/wiki/Steve_Parkin "Steve Parkin") to [Barnsley](/wiki/Barnsley_F.C. "Barnsley F.C.") as his assistant and deciding that he was not capable of playing second-tier football. [[22]](https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/football/football-league/parkins--mission-at-barnsley-616465.html)[*[dead link](/wiki/Wikipedia:Link_rot "Wikipedia:Link rot")*]

19 February 2002: [Roberto Di Matteo](/wiki/Roberto_Di_Matteo "Roberto Di Matteo"), 31-year-old midfielder who was Chelsea's record signing for £4.9 million in 1996 and scored for them in [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") final wins in 1997 and 2000, retires after 17 months out of action having failed to recover from a broken leg suffered in a UEFA Cup game in September 2000. [[23]](https://web.archive.org/web/20110606143010/http://www.thisislondon.co.uk/sport/article-355199-chelsea-star-di-matteo-retires.do)

21 April 2002: [Stuart Pearce](/wiki/Stuart_Pearce "Stuart Pearce"), 40-year-old left back who had been in league football since 1983 and was an England international for more than a decade until his last appearance in 2000, retires at the end of an illustrious career with ended on a high as he helped Manchester City win promotion to the Premier League as Division One champions. His final game was against Portsmouth at [Maine Road](/wiki/Maine_Road "Maine Road"), in which he missed a penalty that if scored would have been the 100th goal of his professional career that had also taken in spells with Coventry City, Nottingham Forest, Newcastle United and West Ham United. [[24]](https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/football/football-league/pearce-misses-penalty-even-after-beasants-friendly-word-657907.html)[*[dead link](/wiki/Wikipedia:Link_rot "Wikipedia:Link rot")*]

10 May 2002: [Lee Dixon](/wiki/Lee_Dixon "Lee Dixon"), 38-year-old right back who signed for Arsenal in January 1988 and played more than 600 games for them, retires after winning his second double in five seasons (and his fourth league title) for the North London club. [[25]](https://archive.today/20130421094411/http://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/football/leagues/premierleague/arsenal/3027501/Dixon-opts-to-retire-by-signing-off-in-style.html)

11 May 2002: [Matthew Le Tissier](/wiki/Matthew_Le_Tissier "Matthew Le Tissier"), 33-year-old striker who spent his entire professional career at [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton_F.C. "Southampton F.C.") since 1986, retires after scoring 162 goals for them but never managing to win a major trophy or play in a European competition, though he did manage several England appearances in the 1990s. [[26]](http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/football/eng_prem/1981889.stm)

## Deaths

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2001%E2%80%9302_in_English_football&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Deaths")]

* 3 July 2001: [Billy Liddell](/wiki/Billy_Liddell "Billy Liddell"), 79, legendary [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") winger who scored 228 goals in all competitions at the club between 1938 and 1961, although the only major trophy he won with them was the league title in 1947. He was capped 28 times by [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_football_team "Scotland national football team"). During the final years of his life, he was ill with [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease") and [Alzheimer's disease](/wiki/Alzheimer%27s_disease "Alzheimer's disease").
* 5 August 2001: [Aaron Flahavan](/wiki/Aaron_Flahavan "Aaron Flahavan"), 25, [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth_F.C. "Portsmouth F.C.") goalkeeper who had played 93 first-team games since 1996, died in a car crash in west Hampshire just before the start of the season.
* 8 August 2001: [Paul Vaessen](/wiki/Paul_Vaessen "Paul Vaessen"), 39, former [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") striker whose career was cut short by injury. Most famous for scoring the goal that earned Arsenal a place in the 1980 Cup Winners' Cup final.
* 19 August 2001: [Les Sealey](/wiki/Les_Sealey "Les Sealey"), 43, who kept goal for [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") in their 1990 FA Cup and 1991 Cup Winners Cup triumphs, died of a heart attack in north London. He had also played for Coventry City, Luton Town, Aston Villa, Birmingham City (on loan), Blackpool and West Ham United, and at the time of his death had been working for West Ham United as goalkeeping coach.
* 20 August 2001: [Tom Staniforth](/wiki/Gordon_Staniforth#son "Gordon Staniforth"), 20, [Sheffield Wednesday](/wiki/Sheffield_Wednesday "Sheffield Wednesday") defender, collapsed and died on a night out in York. He was a promising young defender for the club and a son of former footballer Gordon Staniforth.
* 1 September 2001: [Brian Moore](/wiki/Brian_Moore_(commentator) "Brian Moore (commentator)"), 69, former [BBC Radio](/wiki/BBC_Radio "BBC Radio") and [ITV](/wiki/ITV_(TV_network) "ITV (TV network)") commentator, died of cancer three years after retiring from football commentary.
* 13 September 2001: [Alex Scott](/wiki/Alex_Scott_(footballer_born_1937) "Alex Scott (footballer born 1937)"), 63, played 149 league games as a right-winger for [Everton](/wiki/Everton_F.C. "Everton F.C.") after signing from [Rangers](/wiki/Rangers_F.C. "Rangers F.C.") in February 1963, helping them win the league title that spring and the FA Cup three years later. He returned to his native [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") in 1967 and was capped 16 times for the national side, scoring five goals.
* 17 September 2001: [Ray Gill](/wiki/Ray_Gill "Ray Gill"), 76, the record [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League") appearance holder for [Chester City](/wiki/Chester_City_F.C. "Chester City F.C.").
* 22 October 2001: [Bertie Mee](/wiki/Bertie_Mee "Bertie Mee"), 80, [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") [double](/wiki/Double_(association_football) "Double (association football)")-winning manager 1970–71, died after a long illness. He also guided Arsenal to European Fairs Cup glory, achieved one year before the double triumph. From 1976 to 1986 he was assistant manager to Graham Taylor at Watford, and remained at the club as a director until his retirement in 1991.
* 23 October 2001: [Ken Aston](/wiki/Ken_Aston "Ken Aston"), 86, [World Cup](/wiki/Football_World_Cup "Football World Cup") [referee](/wiki/Referee_(association_football) "Referee (association football)") who created the red and yellow cards system.
* 2 January 2002: [Charlie Mitten](/wiki/Charlie_Mitten "Charlie Mitten"), 80, was a left winger in Manchester United's FA Cup winning team of [1948](/wiki/1948_FA_Cup_Final "1948 FA Cup Final"), before transferring to [Club Santa Fe](/wiki/Club_Santa_Fe "Club Santa Fe"), Colombia, in 1951, and then returning to England to play for Fulham before retiring as a player in 1956. He later managed Mansfield Town and non-league [Altrincham](/wiki/Altrincham_F.C. "Altrincham F.C.").[[11]](#cite_note-14)
* 19 January 2002: [Jeff Astle](/wiki/Jeff_Astle "Jeff Astle"), 59, [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C.") and [England](/wiki/English_national_football_team "English national football team") striker, died suddenly in Burton-upon-Trent. He is best remembered for his appearances on the TV series *Fantasy Football* and for scoring Albion's winning goal against Everton in the 1968 FA Cup final.
* 4 February 2002: [John Bromley](/w/index.php?title=John_Bromley_(television)&action=edit&redlink=1 "John Bromley (television) (page does not exist)"), 68, journalist & TV executive at ITV who ended BBC's monopoly of sports programming with *[World of Sport](/wiki/World_of_Sport_(UK_TV_series) "World of Sport (UK TV series)")*, he is also credited with creating the hit football programme *[Saint and Greavsie](/wiki/Saint_and_Greavsie "Saint and Greavsie")* with Ian St. John & Jimmy Greaves.
* 16 February 2002: Sir [Walter Winterbottom](/wiki/Walter_Winterbottom "Walter Winterbottom"), 88, the first [England](/wiki/English_national_football_team "English national football team") manager. Managed England from 1946 until 1962, when he made way for Alf Ramsey.
* 26 March 2002: [Kenneth Wolstenholme](/wiki/Kenneth_Wolstenholme "Kenneth Wolstenholme"), 81, [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") television commentator, who famously commentated England's 1966 World Cup triumph and is best remembered for saying 'They think it's all over ... it is now' when Geoff Hurst scored England's last-minute winner.
* 9 April 2002: [Roy Dwight](/wiki/Roy_Dwight "Roy Dwight"), 69, played on the right wing for [Nottingham Forest](/wiki/Nottingham_Forest_F.C. "Nottingham Forest F.C.") in their 1959 FA Cup triumph, but was stretchered off in the final against [Luton Town](/wiki/Luton_Town_F.C. "Luton Town F.C.") with a broken leg. He also played for [Fulham](/wiki/Fulham_F.C. "Fulham F.C.") and later [Coventry City](/wiki/Coventry_City_F.C. "Coventry City F.C.") and [Millwall](/wiki/Millwall_F.C. "Millwall F.C.") in a career which yielded 85 league goals in 154 games. He was the uncle of pop legend and [Watford](/wiki/Watford_F.C. "Watford F.C.") chairman [Elton John](/wiki/Elton_John "Elton John").
* 16 April 2002: [Billy Ayre](/wiki/Billy_Ayre "Billy Ayre"), 49, former [Halifax](/wiki/Halifax_Town_A.F.C. "Halifax Town A.F.C."), [Blackpool](/wiki/Blackpool_F.C. "Blackpool F.C."), [Scarborough](/wiki/Scarborough_F.C. "Scarborough F.C."), [Southport](/wiki/Southport_F.C. "Southport F.C.") and [Cardiff City](/wiki/Cardiff_City_F.C. "Cardiff City F.C.") manager, died after a long battle with cancer of the lymph node. He guided Blackpool to two successive [Wembley](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium_(1923) "Wembley Stadium (1923)") play-off finals, in [1990–91](/wiki/1990%E2%80%9391_in_English_football "1990–91 in English football") and [1991–92](/wiki/1991%E2%80%9392_in_English_football "1991–92 in English football").
* 21 May 2002: [Roy Paul](/wiki/Roy_Paul "Roy Paul"), 82, played 270 league games in defence for [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C.") during the 1950s after signing from [Swansea](/wiki/Swansea_City_A.F.C. "Swansea City A.F.C."), and collected an FA Cup winner's medal in 1956. He was capped 33 times by [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_football_team "Wales national football team").
* 28 May 2002: [Wimbledon FC](/wiki/Wimbledon_FC "Wimbledon FC"), 114, played 1,052 Football League games between 1977 and 2002 and won the FA Cup in 1988.
